The next core discipline involves gathering testimonials. You're going to use these testimonials in all kinds of marketing collateral for your client or for yourself. It's a really huge part of marketing because as Ray have said and I've said in previous modules it adds a lot of authority and a sense of competence for your customer or for your own business. So here's some ideas around gathering testimonials. The goal of a testimonial is to overcome resistance or celebrate a success or demonstrate empathy or authority or demonstrate the avoidance of failure. So when you're gathering these testimonials you're not just saying, hey, what did you think of this product or service? You're actually looking for little sound bites that do one of these things, overcome resistance. That is if there's some sort of thing that's making people not want to buy, the price is too high or maybe it's too complicated or I think they're not smart enough to use the product. You want a testimonial that says, I really thought this was going to be difficult to use but it was so easy to use, something like that, right, that helps overcome a resistance or celebrates a success that people say, man, I lost 50 pounds using this product. It was amazing or help demonstrate empathy or authority for the client, for the brand. This brand really cared about me and they've helped so many people. I'm so glad they were able to help me too. Something like that, empathy and authority. You want to put that in the mouth of the client. Using the avoidance of failure is also a great thing in a testimonial. If somebody said, I struggled with this for years but because of ACME so and so, I no longer struggle with that at all. That's the sort of thing that we're looking for for testimonials. Don't let the people who are giving you the testimonials say all the words. We want to ask them the right questions. We want to feed them the right data so that they can give us the response that we are looking for. Okay, here are some rules about testimonials. The first is keep them brief. The second is look for sound bites. The third is capture authenticity. And the fourth is listen from the customer's perspective. You want your sound bites, especially in video and in text also, to be brief. People don't read websites. Remember, they scan them. People really don't watch videos. They're sort of eating dinner. They're walking around. And you want these catchy sound bites to come in. Keep things brief and listen for sound bites. If you're collecting testimonials on video, you're listening. Your ear is listening for that one sound bite that says, I just got a great deal. Or I was so happy with their service. Those are the kinds of things. Think about watching the evening news. If you ever watched the evening news and there's a news story, there was a big fire at such-and-such building and everybody was scared. And then there's one sound bite that said, I heard a woman screaming and I ran over there. And that's it. And you know that that person probably talked for five minutes into the camera. But that news station knows they're just looking for the sound bite that's going to get somebody's attention. That's the sort of thing you want to look for when you're gathering testimonials. Also capture authenticity. We can't actually feed our people the words to say. We're looking to give them the questions that will get the response that we want. But those responses do need to be authentic. And then listen from the customer's perspective. It would be easy for you to listen to a testimonial from your client's perspective. What's going to make your client happy? That's not really our job as copywriters. Our job is to listen to things from the customer's perspective. That is, who is the client trying to motivate? Who is the client trying to engage? That's the person that we want to have in our heads as we're listening for the stories they're going to resonate with the right person. And that is the end user, the customer. Okay. Well, what questions should you ask to get the testimonial that you want? When you go through your Storybrand One page, it's going to generate obvious questions that you want to ask. But let me just give you a few of them so you know. What problem were you dealing with when you called ACME? How was this problem making you feel? That's the internal problem. How did ACME demonstrate that they understood your problem? This is where you're going to populate the one page with empathy. Why did you trust ACME to solve your problem? That's where you're looking for that authority because they've helped thousands of others and because they're clearly the leader in the industry. Did you notice a difference between ACME and the competition? This is going to be really great because you're looking for that differentiator. If you can get a soundbite saying they were just so much more punctual or their prices were so much more fair or they clearly had better quality than some other company, that's the soundbite that we're looking for. What surprised you about working with ACME? This is sort of a jack in the box, right? You don't know what's going to pop up, but it's a great question to ask because you might get a really authentic soundbite and you might get something when people go, wow, that would surprise me. Maybe they gave a money back guarantee or maybe they cleaned your car after they finished working on it, but whatever it is, but something that surprised you is a really great soundbite. And then finally, what does life look like now that ACME has solved your problem? And really dive into this, really get them to respond and try to get a story, a narrative, a visual, something that is tangible that people can go, oh, I want my life to look like that too. That's what you're looking for when you're gathering testimonials. So where do you use testimonials? Well, the answer is anywhere, right? You want to use them on websites. You want to use them in callouts and PDFs. If there's a PDF that's establishing authority or creating reciprocity for one of your clients, get those testimonials and there's those little short brief kind of sound bites of text. Get those scattered throughout this PDF to establish more authority and more trust for your client. You want to use them in an email campaign. You want to use them in live presentations. Get them into that keynote presentation. Even if somebody is giving a keynote, they could say, you know, it's like one of our customers said and that testimonial goes up on the screen, it's a great place to establish authority and trust. You basically just want to get them, as I said, anywhere you can. Now you can overdo it. I would say in one website and one presentation, you probably don't want to go more than three. Remember even establishing authority, it's like salt in the recipe. If you put too much, it's not going to taste right. When we keep tooting our own horn and saying all these people have said nice things about us, it gets overblown a little bit and people start thinking, well, are they the hero or am I the hero because they keep talking about themselves a lot. As soon as you established that authority, you can get out. As soon as you establish that trust, you can move on. But there's no question testimonials are a powerful way to establish that authority. It's a skill that we want to master and we want to learn. It's one great way. You don't even have to really do the copywriting. You have to do the copy listening. You're listening for those brief sound bites and you're populating your clients, marketing collateral with those sound bites. It's going to add a lot of spice to your work and it's going to get a great response for your client.
